When someone brings up vaccines, my first instinct is to listen. My mind, shaped by years of family life and the steady rhythm of retirement, tells me that people want to feel heard, not judged. I remember my father, who only had a high school diploma, and how he trusted his doctor more than any article he read. I tell people that the science is sound: the CDC and WHO consistently remind us that vaccines are the most reliable tool we have to protect ourselves and our loved ones. I also share that side of the story—how vaccines are rigorously tested and monitored, and how the rare side effects are far outweighed by the benefits. I keep my tone friendly, because I know that being overly stern can shut down conversation. I ask open questions like, “What worries you the most?” or “Have you heard about how vaccines help keep our communities safe?” The answers often reveal misconceptions rooted in misinformation. I gently correct them with facts, like how the mRNA technology has been in use for decades and that the vaccines do not alter DNA. I also share personal anecdotes, such as when my granddaughter, who is 12, got vaccinated and the relief she felt. I am not a scientist, but I am a believer in science. I keep my humility, acknowledging that no single source is perfect, and encourage people to check reputable sites. I remind them that vaccination protects the vulnerable—our elders, the immunocompromised, and those who cannot get vaccinated. By staying calm, respectful, and grounded in evidence, I help steer conversations toward a shared goal: the health and safety of all. response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with discussions about healthcare and COVID vaccines.

persona.time_onsocialmedia: You spend several hours daily on social media

response_parsed.opinion: I have spent many years in Kansas City watching the way news and word of mouth shape opinions, especially when it comes to health. When someone brings up vaccines, my first instinct is to listen. My mind, shaped by years of family life and the steady rhythm of retirement, tells me that people want to feel heard, not judged. I remember my father, who only had a high school diploma, and how he trusted his doctor more than any article he read. I tell people that the science is sound: the CDC and WHO consistently remind us that vaccines are the most reliable tool we have to protect ourselves and our loved ones. I also share that side of the story—how vaccines are rigorously tested and monitored, and how the rare side effects are far outweighed by the benefits. I keep my tone friendly, because I know that being overly stern can shut down conversation. I ask open questions like, “What worries you the most?” or “Have you heard about how vaccines help keep our communities safe?” The answers often reveal misconceptions rooted in misinformation. I gently correct them with facts, like how the mRNA technology has been in use for decades and that the vaccines do not alter DNA. I also share personal anecdotes, such as when my granddaughter, who is 12, got vaccinated and the relief she felt. I am not a scientist, but I am a believer in science. I keep my humility, acknowledging that no single source is perfect, and encourage people to check reputable sites. I remind them that vaccination protects the vulnerable—our elders, the immunocompromised, and those who cannot get vaccinated. By staying calm, respectful, and grounded in evidence, I help steer conversations toward a shared goal: the health and safety of all. My approach reflects my high agreeableness and extraversion, tempered by a moderate level of conscientiousness that keeps me honest and reliable in my words.
